next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 14

By April the greenhouses had started sweating before breakfast. Condensation ran down the panes in fat, slow tracks, and the first breath inside Greenhouse Three always hit like a hand over the mouth: wet soil, tomato leaf, damp rope, old compost warming up. Professor Sprout had propped two broken panes with mismatched bits of glass and a strip of tin, and new growth was already climbing over the repair as if damage were only another sort of frame.

Nobody spoke much at first. They had trowels in one hand and essays in the other. Someone had abandoned a Transfiguration parchment on the potting bench and pressed a crescent of dirt straight through the title. A pair of third-years on the far side were arguing in undertones about whether the vanishment chart was due Thursday or Friday, with the low, desperate seriousness of people to whom the answer mattered. It still mattered. The castle had not stopped producing deadlines because it was occupied. There was a two-foot essay due on controlled vanishment, and if the school cracked open over their heads the essay would probably still have been due on controlled vanishment.

Owen worked a tangle of seedling roots free of their tray and tried not to think in systems. Spring made systems too easy. Every bench was a proof of persistence. Nasturtiums had gone reckless over one corner, bright enough to look rude. Shrivelfig cuttings that had sat all winter like dead fingers were furred silver-green at the tips. Someone had left the greenhouse door open a fraction, and cold air kept moving across the backs of his wrists i...
open_hooks_to_carry: Open the Departure Chamber through logic established by the anchor tables rather than sudden luck.
Use the star-lens to show predatory attention beyond Earth without collapsing mystery into overdescription.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ow part of him wanted precision, he added, "I know that isn't the useful bit."

Martha nodded. "No. It isn't."

She turned her attention back to the bread crust in her hand, then to the bowl, blowing the skin aside with one tired breath before taking another sip. She did not dismiss him. She did not need to. The whole kitchen had already adjusted around a fact that no longer required discussion. Tallow moved behind the table with his careful cutlery and did not once ask Owen if he wanted more broth. The ovens hissed. Somewhere in the far pantry a stack of plates settled against itself.

Owen looked at Martha's bowl, at the smear of butter on the crust she had not quite managed to spread evenly with one hand, and thought absurdly of all the small things she used to notice first: whether the porridge had caught at the bottom, whether somebody had left a scarf on a damp peg, whether he'd gone a whole day on tea and crusts and called it enough. She had always dragged the world back down to touch and food and bruises before it could float off into talk. He had liked that in her because it saved him trouble. He saw now what else it had been.

He stood.

Neither Martha nor Tallow looked up.

His own bowl sat full on the table, film forming over the broth. He left it there and went out through the scullery door into the colder dark beyond the kitchens, and for the first time since he had found the footnote in the old Binns edition he understood that a piece of work could go forward exactly as planned and still leave something ruined in its wake that no later correctness would mend.
state_deltas_required: The anchor site has yielded a second route, and Owen needs certainty about the scale and timing of the outside threat.
Owen learns that a major magical battle at Hogwarts will brighten the ward crack into a real breach condition.
